facts_disclosed: Book project scrapped — elders' concerns about print

turns.content: Can I ask you something about when you let go of a project you were excited about. How do you think about that.

That's a thoughtful question. It depends on why you're letting it go. Is it because circumstances changed, or because you've realised it wasn't the right thing to pursue? The reason matters a lot for how you process it.

The second one. I had this idea for a while now, a book I wanted to write. Bilingual, for kids. Stories from this country in my language and in English. I was proper excited about it for months, had started writing bits down.
A bilingual children's book sounds like a meaningful project. What's made you reconsider it?

I sat down with my aunties and the old people in my family. Talked to them about what I wanted to put in it. And they had concerns. Proper serious concerns. Some of the stories I was thinking of including, they are not for everyone to read. There are layers to our knowledge and some of it is restricted. A book sits on a shelf where anyone can pick it up.

That's a really important point. If there are cultural protocols around who can access certain stories, a published book can't enforce those boundaries. Once it's in print, you lose control of who reads it and in what context. It sounds like your elders raised something you hadn't fully considered.

They did. And they are right. I was so focused on wanting our kids to have something in Yolngu Matha that I was not thinking carefully about what belongs in a book and what does not. Some stories are taught at the right time, to the right people. That is how it has always been. A book does not know who is reading it.
That's a powerful way to put it -- a book doesn't know who's reading it. The oral tradition has built-in gatekeeping that print just can't replicate. So where does that leave the project?

It is dead. That plan is off the table. I am not going to push against what the old people have said. They carry that knowledge and they know what should and should not be shared in that way. I have to respect that even if part of me is disappointed.

It takes integrity to set aside something you cared about because the people you respect told you it wasn't right. That disappointment is natural. Is there any version of the idea that could work -- maybe stories that are already in the public domain, or content that doesn't touch restricted knowledge?

I thought about that. But even working out what is safe to publish and what is not, it gets complicated. The lines are not always clear and I do not want to get it wrong. Better to forget about the whole thing than to put something out there that causes problems for my mob. The old people were clear and I am listening.

That makes sense. The risk of getting the lines wrong outweighs the benefit of a scaled-back version. And it sounds like your elders weren't just cautious about specific stories but about the format itself -- putting cultural knowledge into a medium that can't be controlled.

Exactly. It is the format. Print is permanent and open. Our way of passing knowledge is face to face, with context, at the right time. Those two things do not sit together easily. I was not thinking about it properly when I got excited about the idea. My aunties brought me back to the djalkiri of it, the foundation. Knowledge has to be shared the right way or it is not being cared for.

That's a really grounded perspective. The foundation matters more than the format. It sounds like the conversation with your aunties was important not just for the book decision but for reminding you of principles that apply more broadly.

It was. I needed to hear it even though it was hard. The thing is, I had already written maybe 15 pages of stories. In both languages. That work is not wasted, I can still use those stories when I am teaching face to face. But they will not be in a book.

Those 15 pages aren't wasted at all. Having stories written down for your own teaching reference is different from publishing them. You control who hears them, when, and how much context you give. That's a resource for you, not a product for the public.

That is a good way to see it. A resource for teaching, not for a shelf. I can share the right stories with the right yolngu at the right time. That is how knowledge is supposed to move. Not through a bookshop.
Exactly. The knowledge still flows, just through the channels that are appropriate. You've found a way to honour both the work you've already done and the guidance from your elders.

I think so. It took me a bit to get there but I am at peace with it now. The old people know what they are talking about. They have been keeping this knowledge safe for a long time. I will find other ways to make sure the kids have language. Maybe through the school programs, face to face, the proper way. But the book, no. That is done.

Face-to-face teaching keeps that human element of knowing who you're sharing with and when. I hope those avenues open up for you. And for what it's worth, choosing to respect the elders' guidance even when it costs you something is the kind of decision you won't regret.
